title: Somatic Therapy for Survivors of Childhood Criticism and Shaming
excerpt: Discover how somatic therapy helps survivors of childhood criticism and shaming heal trapped trauma in the body and reclaim self-worth.
body:
Understanding Childhood Criticism and Shaming as Trauma

Childhood is meant to be a time of safety and growth, but for many, it is overshadowed by harsh criticism and shaming from caregivers. These experiences aren’t just bad memories; they can create deep trauma that embeds itself in the nervous system and body. The constant message “you are not enough” or “you are wrong” can generate a chronic state of threat, activating survival responses that become hardwired.

Bessel van der Kolk, a pioneer in trauma research, emphasizes how trauma is stored not just in memory but in the body. When children repeatedly experience criticism and shame, their nervous system learns to anticipate danger from close relationships. This creates a persistent sense of insecurity and self-doubt, often manifesting as anxiety, depression, or difficulty trusting others later in life.

The Somatic Impact of Criticism and Shame

Shame and criticism are social and emotional wounds, but they also have very real physiological effects. Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory helps explain how the autonomic nervous system responds to relational threat. When shamed or criticized, children’s nervous systems often shift into defensive modes—fight, flight, or freeze. These states can become habitual, leading to somatic symptoms:

  • Chronic muscle tension and pain
  • Digestive problems
  • Difficulty regulating emotions
  • Feeling disconnected from the body

Pat Ogden, a leading figure in somatic psychology, notes that trauma blocks the natural flow of energy and movement in the body, causing “stuckness” where people feel trapped in old patterns of shame or self-criticism. Over time, this can erode self-esteem and lead to internalized negative beliefs about the self.

Why Traditional Talk Therapy May Leave You Feeling Stuck

If you have tried therapy but still feel stuck, it may be because talk therapy alone doesn’t address the somatic imprint of childhood criticism and shame. Trauma is stored in implicit memory—nonverbal, bodily sensations and automatic nervous system responses—which words alone can’t fully reach.

Francine Shapiro’s work with EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing) shows that integrating somatic and sensory processing can unlock trauma that verbal processing misses. Likewise, Peter Levine’s Somatic Experiencing focuses on helping the body complete unresolved defensive responses to trauma.

Simply put, your body holds onto the effects of shaming experiences long after your mind has intellectually processed them. Without attending to bodily sensations and nervous system regulation, healing remains partial.

What Is Somatic Therapy?

Somatic therapy is an approach that uses body awareness and movement alongside traditional psychotherapy to address trauma. It helps survivors of childhood criticism and shaming reconnect with their bodies, regulate their nervous system, and rewrite the implicit patterns that drive self-criticism and shame.

Somatic therapists guide clients to notice physical sensations, breathing patterns, and subtle movements that occur in response to triggers. This grounded, present-moment awareness allows the nervous system to shift out of defensive states and into safety and connection.

Deb Dana, who specializes in applying Polyvagal Theory to clinical practice, teaches that somatic therapy fosters a sense of safety and social engagement—critical for healing relational trauma rooted in shame and criticism.

Core Techniques in Somatic Therapy

  • Body Scan and Awareness: Developing mindful attention to tension, pain, or numbness that relate to past critical experiences.
  • Tracking Sensations: Noticing where shame or self-criticism “lives” in the body, such as the chest or throat.
  • Resourcing: Identifying internal sensations or memories that evoke safety and calm to counterbalance trauma.
  • Movement and Gesture: Using small, intentional body movements to release trapped energy and complete defensive responses.
  • Breath Regulation: Techniques to calm the nervous system and strengthen the vagal brake, promoting relaxation.

How Somatic Therapy Addresses Core Shame and Self-Criticism

Core shame is the toxic belief that one’s self is fundamentally flawed or unworthy. Childhood criticism and shaming often implant this belief, which then fuels a harsh inner critic. Somatic therapy helps survivors recognize that these feelings are not abstract thoughts but embodied experiences.

By working with the body, survivors can begin to:

  • Interrupt the automatic defensive responses triggered by criticism
  • Create new somatic experiences of safety and acceptance
  • Develop compassion for their own bodily responses and emotions
  • Loosen the grip of old shame-based narratives embedded in the nervous system

Gabor Maté, who emphasizes the mind-body connection in trauma, highlights that healing shame involves both understanding its origins and feeling safety within the body. Somatic therapy provides a pathway to this embodied safety.

“Healing trauma isn’t about forgetting what happened. It’s about rewiring the nervous system to feel safe in your own skin.” – Deb Dana

Integrating Somatic Therapy into Your Healing Journey

Survivors of childhood criticism and shaming can benefit from a multi-layered approach that includes somatic therapy. Here are some practical steps to integrate this work:

  • Find a Qualified Somatic Therapist: Look for therapists trained in Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy (Pat Ogden’s model), or Polyvagal-informed approaches.
  • Practice Mindful Body Awareness: Daily check-ins to notice physical sensations without judgment can build nervous system regulation over time.
  • Engage in Nervous System Regulation Exercises: Techniques like deep diaphragmatic breathing, grounding, or gentle movement help shift defensive states.
  • Journal Your Body’s Responses: Writing about how your body reacts to criticism triggers can deepen self-awareness.
  • Use Resources and Books: Supplement therapy with reading from experts like Bessel van der Kolk’s The Body Keeps the Score or Deb Dana’s The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy.

When to Seek Professional Support

Some survivors find self-directed somatic work empowering, but others need professional guidance to navigate intense trauma responses safely. If you experience overwhelming anxiety, dissociation, or re-traumatization during somatic exercises, it’s important to reach out to a trauma-informed somatic therapist.

A skilled therapist can tailor interventions to your nervous system’s current capacity and help you build resilience gradually. Remember, healing is not linear. You may experience setbacks, but the body’s capacity to heal and reorganize is profound.

Conclusion: Reclaiming Your Body and Self-Worth

Surviving childhood criticism and shaming leaves invisible scars that live in the body and nervous system. Somatic therapy offers a powerful, science-backed way to access and heal these wounds beyond words. By tuning into your body’s wisdom, you can dismantle the grip of shame and self-criticism, restore safety in your nervous system, and reclaim a sense of worthiness.

As Bessel van der Kolk states, “The challenge of recovery is to reestablish ownership of your body and your mind—to feel that you are alive in your body.” Somatic therapy guides you there, one breath and sensation at a time.

faqs.q: How does somatic therapy differ from traditional talk therapy?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy focuses on body sensations and nervous system regulation alongside talk therapy, addressing trauma stored in the body that words alone may not reach.

faqs.q: Can somatic therapy help with deep-rooted shame from childhood?
faqs.a: Yes. Somatic therapy helps survivors feel safety in their bodies and gradually loosen the physiological grip of shame and self-criticism.

faqs.q: Is somatic therapy safe for people with severe trauma?
faqs.a: When guided by a trained professional, somatic therapy is safe and can be tailored to your nervous system’s capacity to prevent overwhelm.

faqs.q: What are some simple somatic practices I can do at home?
faqs.a: Practices like mindful body scans, deep breathing, grounding exercises, and gentle movement can help regulate your nervous system daily.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book explaining how trauma is stored in the body and how somatic approaches can aid recovery.

affiliateProducts.title: The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy by Deb Dana
affiliateProducts.description: An accessible guide to using Polyvagal Theory in clinical and self-healing contexts for nervous system regulation.

affiliateProducts.title: Waking the Tiger by Peter Levine
affiliateProducts.description: Introduces Somatic Experiencing and how to release trauma held in the body through awareness and movement.

metaTitle: Somatic Therapy for Childhood Shame Survivors
metaDescription: Heal childhood criticism and shame trauma with somatic therapy. Learn how body-based healing rebuilds safety and self-worth.
author: The Oracle Lover
